So when the acidity level of the soil is neutralized, the product that you're getting out of that, if you're farming, say, for example, sukumawiki and tomatoes, which have been the main, main concern, specifically here in Kirinyaga, with people, consumers complaining that, a, I eat tomatoes and I get a lot of acidity in my body. I eat beans and I get a lot of acidity in my body. So eventually you find that consumers are ignoring these foods not because that they're not nutritious, but because of the effect that is being brought about by the consistent use of chemical fertilizers. So when we are giving them the kijani vermi compost, it's helping neutralize the acidity and also increasing the nutritional levels of the end product, that is the food that the farmers are getting. That way, we are boosting the nutritional value of every individual out there.

entries.speaker: Fred
entries.text: What obstacle do you encounter and how do you manage them?

entries.speaker: Jannifa
entries.text: The main obstacle that we have encountered so far has been lack of awareness out there. You find that, yes, we do have a very, very, very crucial product I.e. the Kinjanivamy compost and also the insect farming as a product in its own own that needs to be introduced at least in every farmer, ama, in every homestead out there. But now there's that lack of awareness. People do not know about insect farming. People do not know how to go about it. They just hear stories. There are those who fear insects. So there's all that huge knowledge gap between what we are doing and getting it to the right market out there. Yeah, I would say that's the main challenge so far.

entries.speaker: Fred
entries.text: And how do you manage that? Challenges.

entries.speaker: Jannifa
entries.text: So how we are managing that is working with micro influencers like Farm with Fred who can be able to push the message out there. We are also doing it in our TikToks and across all our social media pages to be able to share the message out there with people. And we've seen such an improvement with people wanting to come and learn how they can produce the chemical, the organic fertilizer and also how they can lower their chemicals in their soils.

entries.speaker: Fred
entries.text: Whom do you work with and who do you collaborate with?

entries.speaker: Jannifa
entries.text: So we work with farmers, we work with large scale farmers, poultry farmers, pig farmers, and also we do work with agro processors. These are industries that produce, for example, the avocado industries that produce a lot of waste and they do not know how to handle their waste. We normally work with them. So whom we work with is farmers, large scale and small scale farmers so far, and also the agro processing companies.

entries.speaker: Fred
entries.text: What about collaboration? Who do you collaborate with?

entries.speaker: Jannifa
entries.text: So we are currently collaborating with fellow producers, fellow insect producers, through associations so that we can be able to rally and also send the message out there collectively. We also work with the government partly to be able to also send the message to the government that really what our farms or what our soils require is the organic treatment from products like now the Kijanivami compost.

entries.speaker: Fred
entries.text: What is your advice to people who would want to do the same thing like the one you do?

entries.speaker: Jannifa
entries.text: My advice to people who would like.

entries.speaker: Fred
entries.text: Advice to a farmer who would like.

entries.speaker: Jannifa
entries.text: To do whatever you're doing. Yes. So my advice to a farmer who'd like to set up such an insect farm is first learn be trained on how to go about insect farming. I know there are so many YouTube videos out there on how to do it, but you find that people are sending out wrong information out there on YouTube. So get somebody who can be able to train you and take you through each and every stage of insect farming. Or we offer that as doodle masters, and we are so competent at it.

entries.speaker: Fred
entries.text: Thank you.
